title: Occupational Risk? Chinese Presence Surges at Scarborough Shoal | Asia Maritime Transparency Initiative
gist: AMTI's AIS-tracking data shows China Coast Guard presence at Scarborough Shoal hit 933 ship-days in H1 2026 alone, nearly matching all of 2025, with a coordinated 30-nautical-mile CCG perimeter plus 6-8 maritime militia vessels and a since-removed 'research' structure inside the lagoon -- the most intense, systematic Chinese control effort at the shoal since AIS tracking began in 2019, achieved without crossing the red lines of permanent construction or reclamation.

insights.claim: China Coast Guard patrols at Scarborough Shoal reached 933 ship-days in the first half of 2026 alone, nearly matching all of 2025's annual total of 1,099 ship-days.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Confirms 2026 is on pace to roughly double 2025's already-record CCG presence, a hard leading indicator of intensifying South China Sea friction risk.
insights.quote: CCG patrols at Scarborough amounted to 933 ship-days, nearly as much as 2025’s entire annual total of 1,099 ship-days
insights.locator: Through the first six months of 2026

insights.claim: CCG presence at Scarborough has escalated for three straight years: 516 ship-days in 2024, 1,099 in 2025 (already a doubling), and a pace implying a further sharp increase in 2026 based on H1 data.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A multi-year compounding escalation trend, useful for modeling the probability of a Philippine-China incident or crisis event over the next 12 months.
insights.quote: which itself had already doubled from 2024’s total of 516 ship-days

insights.claim: Monthly CCG patrol volume at Scarborough rose from an average of 90 ship-days in the first half of 2025 to 156 ship-days in the first half of 2026, peaking at 216 ship-days in May 2026.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: May 2026's 216-ship-day peak marks the highest recorded monthly intensity -- a concrete threshold for judging whether future months represent further escalation or a plateau.
insights.locator: On a monthly basis, CCG patrol volume increased

insights.claim: CCG ships now coordinate to maintain a continuous approximately 30 nautical mile perimeter patrol around Scarborough Shoal, while a persistent detachment of 6-8 Chinese maritime militia vessels maintains closer-in coverage -- a denser, more systematic control posture than in prior years.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Describes a de facto blockade architecture (outer CCG ring plus inner militia ring) that materially raises the bar for Philippine resupply or fishing access -- a template that could be replicated at other contested features.
insights.locator: Multiple CCG ships were often seen coordinating

insights.claim: Philippine Coast Guard and Bureau of Fisheries and Aquatic Resources patrols near Scarborough also grew 43% year-on-year (30 to 43 ship-days per month), but remain far outnumbered by Chinese vessels.
insights.speaker: CSIS
entities: Bureau of Fisheries and Aquatic Resources
insights.so_what: Manila is scaling up presence but the force-ratio gap versus China is widening in absolute terms, limiting the Philippines' ability to contest the shoal without external support.
insights.locator: Philippine law enforcement patrols near Scarborough

insights.claim: AIS data recorded 112 days of direct CCG-PCG/BFAR interaction in the first half of 2026 (about 19 days per month), including a May 27 encounter between CCG 21563 and the BRP Datu Pagbuaya in which satellite imagery revealed additional Chinese ships not visible on AIS.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The high interaction frequency plus evidence of AIS-dark Chinese vessels both raise the probability of a miscalculation or collision incident that could trigger a bilateral or U.S.-alliance crisis.
insights.locator: Data for the first half of 2026 showed 112 days

themes.theme: Creeping infrastructure short of red lines

insights.claim: China installed a 6-by-6-meter floating structure inside Scarborough Shoal's lagoon on May 25, 2026 -- prompting a Philippine diplomatic protest -- which Chinese officials described as a 'temporary research facility' studying the shoal's ecosystem before removing it on June 17.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Tests a new gray-zone tactic (temporary 'research' structures) that lets Beijing probe physical control without triggering the traditional red line of permanent construction -- watch for recurrence or scaling up.
insights.quote: the platform was a “temporary research facility” collecting data on the ecosystem of the shoal
insights.locator: Chinese official sources, including the Chinese embassy

insights.claim: The floating research structure's deployment coincided with the Chinese survey vessel Xiang Yang Hong 33 completing operations at Scarborough after surveying multiple reefs in the Spratly Islands, suggesting a coordinated, systematic Chinese survey campaign across South China Sea features.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Signals China is running a broader multi-feature survey and data-collection program, not an isolated Scarborough incident -- relevant for anticipating similar activity at other Spratly features.
insights.locator: The deployment of the platform coincided with

insights.claim: Beyond the removed research structure, two buoys installed since October 2025 remain at Scarborough Shoal, along with several new floating objects identified by the Philippine Coast Guard in May 2026.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Even after removing the higher-profile structure, China is leaving smaller persistent markers in place -- indicates the de facto footprint is still expanding despite the partial climbdown.
insights.locator: This includes two buoys installed last October

insights.claim: AMTI assesses that the scale of 2026 CCG presence at Scarborough Shoal exceeds anything previously observed in the South China Sea since AMTI began regular AIS tracking in 2019, even as China avoids building a permanent structure or conducting land reclamation.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The headline calibration point: this is a record-breaking, unprecedented escalation by AMTI's own multi-year dataset -- a strong signal for elevated South China Sea risk premia and closer U.S.-Philippines alliance monitoring.
insights.quote: The level of concerted CCG presence at the shoal in 2026 is beyond any previously observed CCG activity in the South China Sea since AMTI began regular AIS tracking in 2019.
insights.locator: China’s activities at Scarborough have entered

top_entities: Bureau of Fisheries and Aquatic Resources
